execution_info.prompt_template:
Now you are an expert who is good at using commonsense for reasoning.
The following conversation noted between ’### ###’ involves several speakers.
###
{history}
###
Based on the above historical utterances, please use commonsense to infer the reaction of potential listeners in < "{speaker_id}" : "{utterance}" >.
Output no more than 10 words:

inputs.history:
M: What I want you to do is I want you to write three of those, as if I were three people.  Okay.  You can write -- you can just put my name on all --
F: I can't do that.
M: But what I'm telling you is that's what you're going to do.
F: Uh, no, actually, no I'm not.
M: No, no, because, look, I don't care what your policy says.  I mean--
F: I'm very sorry.  I understand you're frustrated. You just got off of a flight.
M: You understand that the contents of my bag- The contents of my bag cost a hundred and fifty dollars.
M: You--
F: We'll cover fifty.
M: No.  No.  No.  What I need you to do -- I need you to write checks for -- I need you to write three of them. That'll be satisfactory.
M: Just -- you can fill out the form--We can fill out - no, no, no, we can fill out the form three times.
F: We only cover fifty do-
M: And then that'll be that. It's going to be fine. Just-
F: We only cover fifty dollars worth of lost items.
M: I'm going to get very upset.  Like, I mean, I'm- I'm upset right now but I'm going to get really upset if you- if you don't do the check thing.
F: I'm very sorry.
F: I'm very sorry.  I understand you're upset.  You just got off a long flight.
M: You don't understand. You don't understand, actually.  You don't understand because it was my luggage that was lost.  Okay.
F: We really -- we want to work with you. We really -- we want to help.
F: I understand that.  I would know if it was mine. Um-
M: Yeah.  If it was yours, but it's not. It's mine.  Okay. So just--
inputs.utterance: Yeah.  If it was yours, but it's not. It's mine.  Okay. So just--

dev.output: Listeners would likely find M entitled, unreasonable, and frustrating.

inputs.history:
M: Alright, so you're not going to believe this.  But I went home today. I looked through the mail.  And I got an acceptance letter from U.S.C..
M: I'm going to U.S.C.. Yeah.
F: What, no way.
F: Really?
M: Yeah, yeah.
F: I didn't even
F: know you applied to U.S.C..
M: I did.  I did. I've -- yeah, I applied to U.S.C..  I mean, it's really expensive but
M: it's a really good school.  So
M: Um, um- yeah, I'm just -- I'm really excited 'cause I don't know.  It's like a whole -- I mean, it's just like, I've been really -- I wanted to go there ever since I was a kid.  So now I actually am and it's awesome.  It's a really nice campus.  Oh, you've been there.
F: That's amazing.  I've seen it on TV. Um, Wow.  I mean, well what are you going to major in?  What are you going to do?
M: Um, I'm thinking a history and geology minor.
F: Wow.
M: Yeah.  I like to know about history, 'cause, you know, it's- it's just so relevant to the present.
F: Right.
inputs.utterance: Right.

dev.output: Listeners would likely agree or nod in understanding.

inputs.history:
M: Certainly you must.  We're figures of fun alright.
F: How long will it last, this ludicrous and overbearing love of ours?
M: Who knows.
F: Shall we always want to bicker and fight?
M: No.  That fire will fade along with our passion.
F: Oh, dear.  Should we like that.
M: It all depends on how well we've played.
F: What happens if one of us dies?  Does the one that's left, still laugh?
M: Yes.  Yes, with all his might.
F: Well that's serious enough, isn't it?
M: No, no, it isn't.  Death's very laughable; such a cunning little mystery, all done with mirrors.
F: Darling, I believe you're talking nonsense.
M: So is everyone else in the long run.  Let's be superficial and pity the poor philosophers.  Let's blow trumpets and squeakers and- and enjoy the party as much as we can, like very small, quite idiotic little school children.
M: Let's savor the delight of the moment.  Come and kiss me, darling, before your body rots and worms pop in and out of your eye sockets.
F: Elliot, worms don't pop.
M: I don't mind what you do, see?  You can paint yourself bright green all over and dance naked in the Place Vendome and rush off madly with all the men in the world and I shan't say a word as long as you love me best.
F: Thank you, dear.  The same applies to you, except that if I catch you so much as looking at another woman, I will kill you.
M: Do you remember that awful scene we had in Venice?
F: Which particular one?
M: The one when you bought that little painted wooded snake and put it on my bed?
F: Oh, Charles; that was his name, Charles.  Oh, he did wriggle so beautifully.
inputs.utterance: Oh, Charles; that was his name, Charles.  Oh, he did wriggle so beautifully.

dev.output: Amused by her quirky, fond recollection of the snake.

inputs.history:
F: Every day since.
M: Anne, why didn't you let me know?
F: I was waiting for you, Chris.  'Til then, you never wrote and when you did, what did you say?  You sure can be ambiguous, you know.
M: Anne, give me a kiss.  Give me a -
M: I kissed you.  I kissed Annie.  How long, how long I've waited to kiss you.
F: I'll never forgive you.  Why did you wait all these years?  All I've done is sit around and wonder if I was crazy for thinking of you.
M: Anne, we're going to live now.  I'm going to make you so happy.
F: Not like that you're not.
M: I kissed you.
F: Like Larry's brother.  Do it like you, Chris.  What is it, Chris?
M: Let's drive somewhere.  I want to be alone with you.
F: No, what is it, Chris?  Your mother?
M: No, no, nothing like that.
F: Then what's wrong.  Even in your letters, there was something ashamed.
M: Yes, I suppose there has been.  But it's going from me.
F: You've got to tell me.
M: I don't know where to start.
F: It wouldn't work this way.
M: It's all mixed up with so many other things.  You remember overseas, I was in command of a company?
F: Yeah, sure.
M: Well I lost them.
inputs.utterance: Well I lost them.

dev.output: Shock, sadness, and deep concern for M's tragic past.

inputs.history:
F: How do you know she'll marry you?  Maybe she feels the same way father does.
M: Well if she does, then that's the end of it.  From her letters, I think she's forgotten him.  We'll find out.  Then we'll thrash it out with dad. Right, mom?  Don't avoid me.
F: The trouble is, you don't see enough women.  You never did.
M: So what?  I'm not fast with women.
F: I don't see why it has to be Annie.
M: Because it is.
F: Well that's a good answer, but it don't answer anything.  He thinks he's coming back, Chris.  You marry that girl, you're pronouncing him dead.  Now what happens to your father?  Do you know?  Because I don't.
M: Line?
F: Alright, then, mom.
M: Alright, then, mom.  Did we skip something?  No.  Alright, then, mom.
F: Yes we did. Do you want to keep going?
M: Yeah, let's keep going.
F: Just give it some more thought.
M: I've given it three years' of thought.  I'd hope that if
M: father forgot about Larry, we'd have a regular wedding and everything happy.  If I can't get that here, then I'll have to get out.
F: What the hell is this?
M: I'll get out.  I'll get married and live someplace else.  Maybe New York.
F: Are you crazy?
M: I've been a good son too long; a good sucker.  I'm through with it.
F: You've got a business here.  What the hell is this?
M: The business?  The business doesn't inspire me.
inputs.utterance: The business?  The business doesn't inspire me.

dev.output: Shocked, frustrated, and angry at M's defiance.

M: All I can do for you right now is give you a hundred dollars.
F: Okay, but that's like half

M: What I can
M: What I can do for you is give you a hundred dollars right now and fill out complaint form
M: which you- which you'll have to go into a local Verizon dealer--  or Sprint.
F: Okay, no, look, I- I- I- I'm really -- I'm trying to be patient.  I know this isn't your fault.  I know that you didn't do it
F: but, at the same time, I have been through an hour of little robot voices telling me what I needed to do.
F: And I did everything they said.  And now i'm talking to you and you're supposed to put the money back on my account because I didn't make the calls and I'm really tired of dealing with this because I didn't even
M: Ma'am, I understand that.
M: Ma'am, I - I - I understand that. I understand that. What I can do for you right now is what I'm telling you is that I can do- I can give you a hundred dollars back and then you'll have to fill out a complaint form and then you'll get -- there'll be some investigation. Some men will show up at your house and go through your things.
F: Excuse me?
inputs.utterance: Excuse me?

inputs.history:
M: I'm not the only one.  Look at these people.
F: Sure.  They're all told the same story.  It's what keeps us coming back for more; keeps us excited; keeps us thinking that life is going to start any minute now if we could just find the right spot; get in on the action.
M: Are we talking about the same thing?
F: Well, I'm sorry, but it's still just fish to me.
M: Carla, can I ask you something?  Don't take this the wrong way, but are you having your period?
F: Goddamn it, Augie, don't ask me that.  I hate it when you ask me that.  You always ask me that.  It's insulting.
M: Well what's bugging you?
F: We just have different ideas about what this evening is supposed to look like.
M: There's nothing to it.  We come down here.  The grunions arrive.  They do their little fish business.  We go ooh, ah, look at the fish.  They go home.  We go home.
F: For heaven's sake, we are not going to see the grunions.
M: Why not?
F: We never do, do we?  Have we ever?
M: We've missed them twice.
M: That's not ever.
F: Twice is every time we've tried.  That's ever.
M: We'll see them this year.
F: No, we won't.  It's pointless.  It's like waiting up to see Santa Claus.  I feel as though my entire life is going to be spent standing on the beach with my eyes wide open and my hands clasped expectantly waiting for the fish to show up.  And the fish won't show up.
M: I thought you wanted to see them.
F: You don't have any idea what I'm saying.
M: No, of course, not.
F: I did want to see it.  I always do.  I keep thinking this time will be the time, but it never is.
inputs.utterance: I did want to see it.  I always do.  I keep thinking this time will be the time, but it never is.
